title: Alaska Native Settlement Trust Eligibility Act
short_title: Alaska Native Settlement Trust Eligibility Act
plain_summary: This law helps Alaska Natives who are elderly, blind, or disabled get government benefits more easily. Sometimes Alaska Natives get money from special settlement trusts, and this money used to count against them when they applied for help like food stamps or other aid programs. Now for the next five years, this settlement trust money will not count as income when the government decides if someone can get benefits. This means more Alaska Natives who need help will be able to get it without losing their settlement payments.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Alaska Native Settlement Trust Eligibility Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Eligibility for Certain Programs
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. The change affects how certain payments are counted when the government decides if someone qualifies for benefit programs like food stamps or Medicaid. Under this new rule, if an Alaska Native person or their descendant is aged, blind, or disabled, then money they receive from a Settlement Trust will not count against them when applying for government benefits. A Settlement Trust is a special type of fund set up to benefit Alaska Native people. The aged, blind, or disabled definition comes from Social Security rules. This special protection for Settlement Trust payments will last for five years starting from when this law goes into effect.
